Example: void loop() { float tempC = bme.readTemperature(); float humidity = bme.readHumidity(); float pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0; Serial.print(tempC, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(humidity, 1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(pressure_hPa, 2); delay(500); } If you also want Fahrenheit, you can add a 4th column, but that may clutter the plot. Often you plot tempC and optionally compute tempF externally. But if you want it: Serial.print(tempC,2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(tempF,2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(humidity,1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(pressure_hPa,2); If you tell me which Arduino IDE version you’re on, I can suggest the most compatible output format.

I’ll keep it tab separated. Now I’m thinking about the whole weather station: I want to log data to an SD card too. I have an SD module. How do I combine SD logging with the BME280? Anything to watch out for?

You can combine them fine, but there are a few common pitfalls: SPI pin conflicts, chip select lines, power/logic levels, and making sure you flush/close files properly. 1) Bus overview - BME280 currently on I2C (A4/A5). Good—no conflict with SD. - SD modules typically use SPI on Uno: - SCK = D13 - MISO = D12 - MOSI = D11 - CS (chip select) = often D10 (but can be any digital pin) 2) Wiring SD module (typical) - VCC -> 5V if the SD module has a regulator/level shifting; otherwise 3.3V (SD cards are 3.3V logic) - GND -> GND - SCK -> D13 - MISO -> D12 - MOSI -> D11 - CS -> D10 (or whatever your module uses) Important: If you use hardware SPI on Uno, keep D10 as OUTPUT even if you use a different CS pin. This keeps the Uno’s SPI hardware in master mode. 3) Libraries - <SD.h> for basic SD logging (uses SPI) - <SPI.h> is pulled in by SD.h 4) File strategy For reliability: - Open the file, write a line, close it each time (simpler, safer but more wear and slower). - Or keep it open and flush periodically (faster but risk data loss if power drops). For a beginner weather station, open/write/close per sample is okay if you log every few seconds or longer. 5) Example combined sketch (tab/CSV) This logs CSV to the SD and prints tab-separated to Serial Plotter. #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <SD.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; const uint8_t BME_ADDR = 0x76; const int SD_CS_PIN = 10; File logFile; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} Wire.begin(); if (!bme.begin(BME_ADDR)) { Serial.println("BME280 not found"); while (1); } pinMode(10, OUTPUT); // important for SPI master mode on Uno if (!SD.begin(SD_CS_PIN)) { Serial.println("SD init failed"); while (1); } // Create/open log file logFile = SD.open("weather.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (logFile) { // Optional: write header if file is empty if (logFile.size() == 0) { logFile.println("ms,tempC,humidity,pressure_hPa"); } logFile.close(); } else { Serial.println("Could not open weather.csv"); while (1); } Serial.println("Ready"); } void loop() { unsigned long ms = millis(); float tempC = bme.readTemperature(); float humidity = bme.readHumidity(); float pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0; // Serial Plotter (tab-separated) Serial.print(tempC, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(humidity, 1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(pressure_hPa, 2); // SD log (CSV) logFile = SD.open("weather.csv", FILE_WRITE); if (logFile) { logFile.print(ms); logFile.print(","); logFile.print(tempC, 2); logFile.print(","); logFile.print(humidity, 1); logFile.print(","); logFile.println(pressure_hPa, 2); logFile.close(); } else { Serial.println("SD write error"); } delay(2000); } 6) Things to watch out for - Power: SD cards can draw bursts of current; weak 3.3V regulators can cause brownouts. Many SD modules accept 5V and have onboard regulation. - Level shifting: If your SD module is “bare” (no level shifters), driving it with 5V SPI can corrupt it or cause flaky behavior. - Long wires: SPI is more sensitive; keep wires short. - File names: 8.3 format on SD library (WEATHER.CSV is fine; "weather.csv" usually works but under the hood it’s 8.3). - Timing: if you log very frequently (e.g., 10 Hz), open/close each loop becomes slow. If you tell me your SD module’s pin labels (VCC/3V3? CS? etc.), I can sanity-check the level shifting/regulator situation.
The SD module says VCC, GND, MISO, MOSI, SCK, CS. It’s the common blue one. I think it has a regulator.

That common blue module usually has a 3.3V regulator and resistor dividers for level shifting on MOSI/SCK/CS (not always ideal but generally works at Uno speeds). MISO is typically 3.3V output which the Uno reads fine. A couple practical tips with that module: - Use VCC to 5V, not 3.3V (the onboard regulator needs headroom). - Keep SPI wires short. - If SD init fails intermittently, try lowering SPI speed (SD.begin(csPin, SPI_HALF_SPEED) exists in some libs/cores, but not all; another approach is using SdFat library for better control). Also, since you’re using Serial Plotter, consider making Serial output very regular (no extra debug lines in loop), otherwise plots get messy. Keep debug prints in setup only. If you’d like, we can add: - A sample interval constant - A simple rolling average for smoother plots - A “log every N seconds” schedule using millis() instead of delay() (so it’s easier to add LCD/buttons later)
Yeah I want to avoid delay() because I’ll add buttons later. Can you show a millis-based loop that reads every 2 seconds and logs, but still allows other code to run?

Use a non-blocking scheduler pattern: store the last sample time and check elapsed time each loop. Here’s a structured sketch: #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <SD.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; const uint8_t BME_ADDR = 0x76; const int SD_CS_PIN = 10; const unsigned long SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS = 2000; unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} Wire.begin(); if (!bme.begin(BME_ADDR)) { Serial.println("BME280 not found"); while (1); } pinMode(10, OUTPUT); if (!SD.begin(SD_CS_PIN)) { Serial.println("SD init failed"); while (1); } File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { if (f.size() == 0) { f.println("ms,tempC,humidity,pressure_hPa"); } f.close(); } else { Serial.println("Failed to open log file"); while (1); } Serial.println("Ready"); } void loop() { unsigned long now = millis(); // Other non-blocking tasks can go here: // - read buttons // - update LCD // - handle serial commands if (now - lastSampleMs >= SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS) { lastSampleMs = now; takeSampleAndLog(now); } } void takeSampleAndLog(unsigned long ms) { float tempC = bme.readTemperature(); float humidity = bme.readHumidity(); float pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0; // Serial Plotter output (tab-separated, no extra text) Serial.print(tempC, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(humidity, 1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(pressure_hPa, 2); File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { f.print(ms); f.print(","); f.print(tempC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(humidity, 1); f.print(","); f.println(pressure_hPa, 2); f.close(); } } This pattern: - Never blocks waiting for time to pass - Keeps the loop spinning fast, so button reads feel responsive - Makes it easy to add multiple timed tasks (e.g., LCD refresh every 250 ms, SD log every 2000 ms) If you plan to add debounced buttons, we can add a simple debounce function next.
I do want buttons: maybe one to toggle units C/F and one to start/stop logging. How would you structure that? I’m not using interrupts yet.

Keep it simple: poll buttons each loop, debounce them, and detect “press events” (edge from not-pressed to pressed). Then update state variables like useFahrenheit and loggingEnabled. Assumptions: - Two momentary buttons wired from pin to GND - Use internal pull-ups (so idle reads HIGH, pressed reads LOW) Wiring: - Button1 between D2 and GND - Button2 between D3 and GND Code structure: - A small Button struct with debounce timing - On press event: toggle a boolean Example: #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <SD.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; const uint8_t BME_ADDR = 0x76; const int SD_CS_PIN = 10; const int BTN_UNIT_PIN = 2; const int BTN_LOG_PIN  = 3; bool useFahrenheit = false; bool loggingEnabled = true; const unsigned long SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS = 2000; unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; struct DebouncedButton { int pin; bool stableState;              // HIGH/LOW bool lastReading; unsigned long lastChangeMs; }; const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 30; DebouncedButton btnUnit {BTN_UNIT_PIN, HIGH, HIGH, 0}; DebouncedButton btnLog  {BTN_LOG_PIN,  HIGH, HIGH, 0}; void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} pinMode(BTN_UNIT_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); pinMode(BTN_LOG_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); Wire.begin(); if (!bme.begin(BME_ADDR)) while (1); pinMode(10, OUTPUT); if (!SD.begin(SD_CS_PIN)) while (1); File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { if (f.size() == 0) { f.println("ms,tempC,tempF,humidity,pressure_hPa,logging"); } f.close(); } } void loop() { unsigned long now = millis(); if (buttonPressedEvent(btnUnit, now)) { useFahrenheit = !useFahrenheit; } if (buttonPressedEvent(btnLog, now)) { loggingEnabled = !loggingEnabled; } if (now - lastSampleMs >= SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS) { lastSampleMs = now; sampleOutputAndMaybeLog(now); } // other tasks... } bool buttonPressedEvent(DebouncedButton &b, unsigned long now) { bool reading = digitalRead(b.pin); if (reading != b.lastReading) { b.lastChangeMs = now; b.lastReading = reading; } if ((now - b.lastChangeMs) > DEBOUNCE_MS) { if (reading != b.stableState) { b.stableState = reading; // pressed event when stable goes HIGH->LOW (because pull-up) if (b.stableState == LOW) return true; } } return false; } void sampleOutputAndMaybeLog(unsigned long ms) { float tempC = bme.readTemperature(); float tempF = tempC * 9.0/5.0 + 32.0; float humidity = bme.readHumidity(); float pressure_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0; float tempOut = useFahrenheit ? tempF : tempC; // Serial Plotter: keep consistent columns // Column1: temp (in selected units) // Column2: humidity // Column3: pressure Serial.print(tempOut, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(humidity, 1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(pressure_hPa, 2); if (!loggingEnabled) return; File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { f.print(ms); f.print(","); f.print(tempC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(tempF, 2); f.print(","); f.print(humidity, 1); f.print(","); f.print(pressure_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.println(loggingEnabled ? 1 : 0); f.close(); } } Notes: - Serial Plotter columns remain consistent even if units toggle; it will just show the temp line jump scale. - SD log stores both C and F regardless of what you’re displaying. - If you later add an LCD, you can display “C” or “F” and “LOG ON/OFF”. If you want to avoid the temp jump in the plot, keep Serial Plotter always in Celsius and only toggle units on the LCD, while SD logs both. That’s often nicer for analysis.
That makes sense. For the LCD, I have a 16x2 with an I2C backpack. Will that conflict with the BME280 on I2C?

It can share the same I2C bus as long as each device has a unique address. That’s one of the benefits of I2C. Typical addresses: - BME280: 0x76 or 0x77 (yours is 0x76) - I2C LCD backpack (PCF8574): often 0x27 or 0x3F (but can vary) So no conflict unless the LCD backpack happens to be 0x76 (very unlikely). What to do: 1) Wire LCD backpack to the same I2C pins: - LCD VCC -> 5V (most backpacks are 5V) - LCD GND -> GND - LCD SDA -> A4 - LCD SCL -> A5 2) Run I2C scanner again with both connected. You should see two addresses (0x76 plus LCD’s address). 3) Library Common library: “LiquidCrystal_I2C” (there are multiple variants). Another reliable one is “hd44780” by Bill Perry (often more compatible). Simple LCD init example (for common LiquidCrystal_I2C library): #include <LiquidCrystal_I2C.h> LiquidCrystal_I2C lcd(0x27, 16, 2); Then in setup: lcd.init(); lcd.backlight(); lcd.setCursor(0,0); lcd.print("Hello"); Potential gotcha: logic levels on I2C - Your BME280 is currently powered at 3.3V. Your LCD backpack likely pulls SDA/SCL up to 5V (through its pull-ups), which would put 5V on the I2C lines—bad for a 3.3V-only BME280 unless your BME breakout has level shifting. Because your BME board has VIN and seems intended for 5V systems, it might include level shifting, but your earlier experience with CS suggests the logic rail matters. Safer approach: - Power the BME280 breakout from 5V (VIN) only if it truly level shifts I2C. But earlier, I2C didn’t work when VIN=5V and CS floating; once configured, it might work at 5V too. The bigger question is whether it tolerates 5V I2C lines. To avoid risking the sensor, you can: - Use an I2C level shifter module between the Uno (5V side) and the BME280 (3.3V side), while LCD stays on 5V side, or - Ensure the I2C pull-ups are to 3.3V, not 5V, which is tricky with a 5V LCD backpack unless you modify it. Before connecting the LCD, can you confirm whether your GY-BME280 board has any level shifting components (often a small regulator and maybe a few MOSFETs or a chip like BSS138)? If you can send a description of components on the board (or a photo), I can tell you if it’s 5V-safe on I2C.
I can’t send a photo right now, but I see a small 3-pin regulator and a few resistors, no obvious MOSFETs. So maybe it’s not level shifting. What’s the safest wiring then?

Safest is to keep the I2C bus at 3.3V levels for the BME280, and not let the LCD backpack pull it up to 5V. Two practical options: Option 1: Use an I2C level shifter (recommended) - Keep Uno + LCD backpack on the 5V side (HV) - Keep BME280 on the 3.3V side (LV) - Put a bidirectional I2C level shifter between them (commonly a BSS138-based module) Wiring (typical level shifter board has HV, LV, GND, and channels like SDA/SCL): - Level shifter HV -> 5V - Level shifter LV -> 3.3V - Level shifter GND -> GND - Uno SDA (A4) -> HV SDA - Uno SCL (A5) -> HV SCL - LCD SDA/SCL -> HV SDA/HV SCL (same as Uno side) - BME SDA/SCL -> LV SDA/LV SCL - BME VIN -> 3.3V - BME GND -> GND This ensures the BME sees only 3.3V I2C. Option 2: Modify pull-ups so I2C is pulled to 3.3V (more fiddly) - You’d need to remove/disable the LCD backpack pull-up resistors to 5V and ensure pull-ups exist to 3.3V. Some LCD backpacks have two pull-up resistors (often 4.7k) to VCC. If you can identify and remove them, you can then add pull-ups to 3.3V. But this is not beginner-friendly. So I’d go with Option 1: buy/use a 2-channel I2C level shifter module. They’re cheap and make mixed-voltage I2C sane. If you want to proceed without extra hardware, the “risky but sometimes works” approach is to power the BME module from 5V (VIN) and assume it has I2C level shifting. But based on your description (regulator + resistors only), I would not assume that.
Ok I’ll get a level shifter. Meanwhile, can we improve accuracy? I read BME280 needs calibration and maybe oversampling. How do I set that, and do I need to calibrate temperature offset?

The BME280 has factory calibration constants stored in the chip; the library uses them automatically. You don’t need to manually calibrate it for basic operation. What you can do: 1) Configure oversampling and filtering Oversampling reduces noise (at the cost of slower measurements). Filtering helps smooth pressure readings. With Adafruit_BME280, you already saw setSampling(). For a weather station (slow-changing), a common configuration is: - Temperature oversampling x2 or x4 - Pressure oversampling x16 (pressure is noisier and used for trends) - Humidity oversampling x1 or x2 - Filter x16 - Standby 1000 ms (or longer) Example: bme.setSampling( Adafruit_BME280::MODE_NORMAL, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X2,   // temp Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X16,  // pressure Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X1,   // humidity Adafruit_BME280::FILTER_X16, Adafruit_BME280::STANDBY_MS_1000 ); If you’re sampling every 2 seconds, standby 1000 ms is fine. 2) Temperature offset Many people apply a small offset because: - The sensor can read slightly high if it’s warmed by the board/regulator/nearby components. - Enclosures can trap heat. There’s no “official” offset; you determine it by comparing against a trusted thermometer under stable conditions. Implementation: float tempC = bme.readTemperature() + tempOffsetC; where tempOffsetC might be -1.0, -0.5, etc. For a beginner build: - Start with offset = 0 - Once you have the station assembled in its final enclosure, compare to a reference for an hour and adjust. 3) Pressure “sea-level” adjustment Pressure varies with altitude. If you want sea-level equivalent pressure (what weather reports use), you need altitude and a formula. The library’s readAltitude() estimates altitude from current pressure given sea-level pressure; the reverse (sea-level pressure from altitude) is also possible. If you know your altitude, you can compute sea-level pressure: p0 = p / pow(1 - altitude/44330.0, 5.255) Where p is station pressure in hPa. But if you don’t know altitude, you can still log station pressure and look at trends—useful for weather changes. 4) Humidity calibration Humidity sensors can have drift; if you care, you can do a simple offset calibration too, but it’s harder to do correctly without a reference. If you want, I can give you: - A “settings” block for oversampling - A structure that applies user-configurable offsets and logs both raw and adjusted values
I want to log both raw and adjusted values, and also compute dew point. Can you add dew point calculation and include that in the SD log?

Yes. Dew point can be computed from temperature and relative humidity. A common approximation is the Magnus formula. For temperature in Celsius and RH in %: gamma = (a*T)/(b+T) + ln(RH/100) dewPoint = (b*gamma)/(a-gamma) with a=17.62, b=243.12 °C (good for typical ambient range) We’ll log: ms, tempC_raw, tempC_adj, hum_raw, hum_adj, press_hPa_raw, press_hPa_adj (if you want), dewPointC Pressure usually doesn’t need an “offset” unless you’re matching another sensor, but you can include a pressure offset too. Here’s an updated sketch core. (I’m focusing on the sampling/logging functions; you can merge with your button code.) Add at top: float tempOffsetC = 0.0; float humOffsetPct = 0.0; float pressOffsethPa = 0.0; Dew point function: float dewPointC(float tempC, float rh) { if (rh <= 0.0) rh = 0.1;     // avoid log(0) if (rh > 100.0) rh = 100.0; const float a = 17.62; const float b = 243.12; float gamma = (a * tempC) / (b + tempC) + log(rh / 100.0); return (b * gamma) / (a - gamma); } Logging function: void sampleOutputAndMaybeLog(unsigned long ms) { float tempC_raw = bme.readTemperature(); float hum_raw = bme.readHumidity(); float press_hPa_raw = bme.readPressure() / 100.0; float tempC_adj = tempC_raw + tempOffsetC; float hum_adj = hum_raw + humOffsetPct; float press_hPa_adj = press_hPa_raw + pressOffsethPa; // Clamp humidity to 0..100 after adjustment if (hum_adj < 0) hum_adj = 0; if (hum_adj > 100) hum_adj = 100; float dewC = dewPointC(tempC_adj, hum_adj); // Serial Plotter: pick a few signals Serial.print(tempC_adj, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(hum_adj, 1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(dewC, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(press_hPa_adj, 2); if (!loggingEnabled) return; File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { f.print(ms); f.print(","); f.print(tempC_raw, 2); f.print(","); f.print(tempC_adj, 2); f.print(","); f.print(hum_raw, 1); f.print(","); f.print(hum_adj, 1); f.print(","); f.print(press_hPa_raw, 2); f.print(","); f.print(press_hPa_adj, 2); f.print(","); f.println(dewC, 2); f.close(); } } And update your CSV header in setup accordingly: "ms,tempC_raw,tempC_adj,hum_raw,hum_adj,press_raw_hPa,press_adj_hPa,dewC" If you also want dew point in Fahrenheit, you can add dewF = dewC * 9/5 + 32.
Nice. How about heat index? I know that’s more for hot days, but I want to learn.

Heat index combines temperature and humidity to estimate “feels like” in warm conditions. The common NOAA formula is typically expressed in Fahrenheit and is valid mainly when: - T >= ~80°F and RH >= ~40% Outside that range it’s less meaningful. We can compute it and log it, but you should treat it as “only valid sometimes.” A simple approach: - Compute tempF and RH - If conditions meet thresholds, compute heat index; otherwise just set it equal to tempF (or leave blank/NaN) NOAA heat index formula (Fahrenheit): HI = -42.379 + 2.04901523*T + 10.14333127*RH - 0.22475541*T*RH - 0.00683783*T*T - 0.05481717*RH*RH + 0.00122874*T*T*RH + 0.00085282*T*RH*RH - 0.00000199*T*T*RH*RH We can implement: float heatIndexF(float tempF, float rh) { float T = tempF; float R = rh; float HI = -42.379 + 2.04901523 * T + 10.14333127 * R - 0.22475541 * T * R - 0.00683783 * T * T - 0.05481717 * R * R + 0.00122874 * T * T * R + 0.00085282 * T * R * R - 0.00000199 * T * T * R * R; return HI; } Then in sampling: float tempF_adj = tempC_adj * 9.0/5.0 + 32.0; float hiF = tempF_adj; if (tempF_adj >= 80.0 && hum_adj >= 40.0) { hiF = heatIndexF(tempF_adj, hum_adj); } Log hiF (and maybe convert back to C if you want): float hiC = (hiF - 32.0) * 5.0/9.0; Add to header and file. If you want an even simpler “feels like” metric across more conditions, wind chill would require a wind sensor, so heat index is the main one you can do with BME280 alone.
Great. Now, I’m noticing my pressure readings jump a bit. I set filter x16. Is there anything else to smooth it? Maybe a moving average?

Yes—pressure noise is normal. You can smooth further with a moving average or exponential smoothing in software. Two common approaches: 1) Simple moving average (SMA) Keep the last N pressure readings and average them. Pros: strong smoothing. Cons: uses RAM and introduces lag. 2) Exponential moving average (EMA) newAvg = alpha*new + (1-alpha)*oldAvg Pros: minimal RAM, easy. Cons: less intuitive window size. On an Uno, EMA is very convenient. Example EMA for pressure: float pressAvg = 0; bool pressAvgInit = false; const float alpha = 0.2; // 0..1, higher = less smoothing When you read press_hPa_adj: if (!pressAvgInit) { pressAvg = press_hPa_adj; pressAvgInit = true; } else { pressAvg = alpha * press_hPa_adj + (1 - alpha) * pressAvg; } Then use pressAvg for display/logging (or log both raw and smoothed). If you sample every 2 seconds: - alpha=0.2 roughly means a time constant of ~ (1/alpha) samples ~5 samples ~10 seconds-ish smoothing. - alpha=0.05 gives stronger smoothing (~40 seconds-ish). If you prefer SMA, N=10 at 2-second sampling gives a 20-second window. Do you want to log raw pressure and smoothed pressure separately? That’s helpful for later analysis.
Yes, log both. Also, can we detect trends like “pressure rising/falling” over the last hour?

On an Uno with limited RAM, you can still do a simple trend estimate without storing every sample for an hour. Two lightweight methods: Method A: Compare current smoothed pressure to value from ~1 hour ago using a ring buffer - If you sample every 2 seconds, 1 hour = 1800 samples. Storing 1800 floats is too much RAM (~7200 bytes just for floats, plus overhead; Uno has 2KB SRAM). But you can downsample: store one value per minute. - 60 minutes => 60 floats => 240 bytes. That’s fine. Approach: - Every minute, store the current smoothed pressure into a ring buffer of length 60. - Trend = current - value_60min_ago (or difference between last and first in buffer) - Then classify: rising/falling/steady based on threshold (e.g., > +0.5 hPa/hour rising, < -0.5 falling) Method B: Linear regression on minute samples Slightly more math, but still feasible. Probably overkill initially. Let’s implement Method A. Data: const int TREND_POINTS = 60; float pressMinute[TREND_POINTS]; int trendIndex = 0; bool trendFilled = false; Timers: - sample every 2 seconds (already) - record minute sample every 60,000 ms using another millis timer Then compute: float pressNow = pressAvg; float pressThen = trendFilled ? pressMinute[trendIndex] : pressMinute[0]; // careful with indexing Simpler: store at trendIndex then advance; the “oldest” is at trendIndex after you advance (ring buffer semantics). We can write a helper. Classification: float delta = pressNow - pressOld; float rate = delta; // hPa per hour since 1 hour window if (rate > 0.5) "RISING" else if (rate < -0.5) "FALLING" else "STEADY" We can log delta_hPa_1h and maybe a numeric trend code. Because Serial Plotter likes numeric columns, log the delta as a number; for LCD you can show text. If you want, I can provide a full integrated sketch with: - Buttons - EMA smoothing - Minute ring buffer trend - SD logging - Serial plot columns

Below is a readable integrated sketch for: - BME280 on I2C address 0x76 - SD module on SPI CS=10 - Two buttons on D2 (unit toggle) and D3 (logging toggle), using INPUT_PULLUP - Sampling every 2 seconds (non-blocking) - Pressure EMA smoothing - One-minute trend buffer (60 minutes) - SD logging with raw + adjusted + smoothed + dew point + heat index + 1-hour pressure change - Serial Plotter outputs 4 numeric columns: tempDisplayed, humidityAdj, pressureSmoothed, pressureDelta1h You can change what Serial Plotter shows by editing the Serial.print section. Code: #include <Wire.h> #include <SPI.h> #include <SD.h> #include <Adafruit_Sensor.h> #include <Adafruit_BME280.h> #include <math.h> Adafruit_BME280 bme; const uint8_t BME_ADDR = 0x76; // SD const int SD_CS_PIN = 10; // Buttons const int BTN_UNIT_PIN = 2; // toggle C/F for display const int BTN_LOG_PIN  = 3; // toggle logging on/off // State bool useFahrenheit = false; bool loggingEnabled = true; // Timing const unsigned long SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS = 2000; unsigned long lastSampleMs = 0; const unsigned long MINUTE_INTERVAL_MS = 60000; unsigned long lastMinuteMs = 0; // Calibration offsets (adjust later if you want) float tempOffsetC = 0.0; float humOffsetPct = 0.0; float pressOffsethPa = 0.0; // Pressure smoothing (EMA) float pressSmooth = 0.0; bool pressSmoothInit = false; const float PRESS_ALPHA = 0.10; // 0..1; lower = more smoothing // 1-hour trend using 1-minute samples const int TREND_POINTS = 60; float pressMinute[TREND_POINTS]; int trendIndex = 0; bool trendFilled = false; // Debounce struct DebouncedButton { int pin; bool stableState; bool lastReading; unsigned long lastChangeMs; }; const unsigned long DEBOUNCE_MS = 30; DebouncedButton btnUnit {BTN_UNIT_PIN, HIGH, HIGH, 0}; DebouncedButton btnLog  {BTN_LOG_PIN,  HIGH, HIGH, 0}; // ---------- Helpers ---------- bool buttonPressedEvent(DebouncedButton &b, unsigned long now) { bool reading = digitalRead(b.pin); if (reading != b.lastReading) { b.lastChangeMs = now; b.lastReading = reading; } if ((now - b.lastChangeMs) > DEBOUNCE_MS) { if (reading != b.stableState) { b.stableState = reading; if (b.stableState == LOW) return true; // pressed } } return false; } float dewPointC_from_T_RH(float tempC, float rh) { if (rh <= 0.0) rh = 0.1; if (rh > 100.0) rh = 100.0; const float a = 17.62; const float b = 243.12; float gamma = (a * tempC) / (b + tempC) + log(rh / 100.0); return (b * gamma) / (a - gamma); } float heatIndexF_NOAA(float tempF, float rh) { float T = tempF; float R = rh; float HI = -42.379 + 2.04901523 * T + 10.14333127 * R - 0.22475541 * T * R - 0.00683783 * T * T - 0.05481717 * R * R + 0.00122874 * T * T * R + 0.00085282 * T * R * R - 0.00000199 * T * T * R * R; return HI; } void ensureLogHeader() { File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { if (f.size() == 0) { f.println("ms,tempC_raw,tempC_adj,tempF_adj,hum_raw,hum_adj,press_raw_hPa,press_adj_hPa,press_smooth_hPa,dewC,dewF,heatIndexF,pressDelta1h_hPa,logging,unitsF"); } f.close(); } } void recordMinutePressure(unsigned long nowMs, float pressSmooth_hPa) { // store current minute sample pressMinute[trendIndex] = pressSmooth_hPa; trendIndex++; if (trendIndex >= TREND_POINTS) { trendIndex = 0; trendFilled = true; } } bool haveOneHourHistory() { return trendFilled; // requires full 60 minutes collected } float pressureDelta1h(float pressNow) { // Oldest value is at trendIndex (the next slot to be overwritten) // When filled, trendIndex points to the oldest sample. if (!trendFilled) return 0.0; float pressOld = pressMinute[trendIndex]; return pressNow - pressOld; } // ---------- Setup/Loop ---------- void setup() { Serial.begin(9600); while (!Serial) {} pinMode(BTN_UNIT_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); pinMode(BTN_LOG_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); Wire.begin(); if (!bme.begin(BME_ADDR)) { Serial.println("BME280 not found"); while (1); } // Sensor sampling configuration bme.setSampling( Adafruit_BME280::MODE_NORMAL, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X2, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X16, Adafruit_BME280::SAMPLING_X1, Adafruit_BME280::FILTER_X16, Adafruit_BME280::STANDBY_MS_1000 ); pinMode(10, OUTPUT); // keep SPI master mode if (!SD.begin(SD_CS_PIN)) { Serial.println("SD init failed"); while (1); } ensureLogHeader(); // Initialize minute timer so first minute record happens after 60s lastMinuteMs = millis(); // Optional: print a one-time line that won't confuse Serial Plotter too much // (If you use Serial Plotter, you may want to comment this out.) // Serial.println("Ready"); } void loop() { unsigned long now = millis(); // Buttons if (buttonPressedEvent(btnUnit, now)) { useFahrenheit = !useFahrenheit; } if (buttonPressedEvent(btnLog, now)) { loggingEnabled = !loggingEnabled; } // Sample task if (now - lastSampleMs >= SAMPLE_INTERVAL_MS) { lastSampleMs = now; takeSample(now); } // Minute trend task if (now - lastMinuteMs >= MINUTE_INTERVAL_MS) { lastMinuteMs += MINUTE_INTERVAL_MS; // keeps better long-term timing than lastMinuteMs = now if (pressSmoothInit) { recordMinutePressure(now, pressSmooth); } } // Other tasks can go here (LCD updates, serial commands, etc.) } void takeSample(unsigned long ms) { // Raw reads float tempC_raw = bme.readTemperature(); float hum_raw = bme.readHumidity(); float press_raw_hPa = bme.readPressure() / 100.0; // Apply offsets float tempC_adj = tempC_raw + tempOffsetC; float hum_adj = hum_raw + humOffsetPct; float press_adj_hPa = press_raw_hPa + pressOffsethPa; if (hum_adj < 0) hum_adj = 0; if (hum_adj > 100) hum_adj = 100; // Smooth pressure if (!pressSmoothInit) { pressSmooth = press_adj_hPa; pressSmoothInit = true; } else { pressSmooth = PRESS_ALPHA * press_adj_hPa + (1.0 - PRESS_ALPHA) * pressSmooth; } // Derived values float tempF_adj = tempC_adj * 9.0/5.0 + 32.0; float dewC = dewPointC_from_T_RH(tempC_adj, hum_adj); float dewF = dewC * 9.0/5.0 + 32.0; float heatIndexF = tempF_adj; if (tempF_adj >= 80.0 && hum_adj >= 40.0) { heatIndexF = heatIndexF_NOAA(tempF_adj, hum_adj); } float delta1h = haveOneHourHistory() ? pressureDelta1h(pressSmooth) : 0.0; // Serial Plotter output: 4 numeric columns, tab-separated float tempDisplayed = useFahrenheit ? tempF_adj : tempC_adj; Serial.print(tempDisplayed, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(hum_adj, 1); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.print(pressSmooth, 2); Serial.print("\t"); Serial.println(delta1h, 2); // SD logging if (!loggingEnabled) return; File f = SD.open("WEATHER.CSV", FILE_WRITE); if (f) { f.print(ms); f.print(","); f.print(tempC_raw, 2); f.print(","); f.print(tempC_adj, 2); f.print(","); f.print(tempF_adj, 2); f.print(","); f.print(hum_raw, 1); f.print(","); f.print(hum_adj, 1); f.print(","); f.print(press_raw_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.print(press_adj_hPa, 2); f.print(","); f.print(pressSmooth, 2); f.print(","); f.print(dewC, 2); f.print(","); f.print(dewF, 2); f.print(","); f.print(heatIndexF, 2); f.print(","); f.print(delta1h, 2); f.print(","); f.print(loggingEnabled ? 1 : 0); f.print(","); f.println(useFahrenheit ? 1 : 0); f.close(); } } A couple notes: - The 1-hour trend will be 0 until you’ve collected a full hour of minute samples. - If you want the trend to start earlier, you can compute over “minutes collected so far,” but then it’s not “per hour.” - Serial Plotter will show delta1h as flat zero for the first hour; that’s expected. If you want, we can add a simple serial command interface (type “TOFF -0.7” to set temp offset, “LOG 0/1”, etc.) so you don’t need to recompile to tweak offsets.
Serial commands would be awesome. I’m not sure how to parse input safely though.

A simple, safe approach on Uno: - Read a line into a small char buffer (e.g., 40–80 chars) - Split tokens by spaces - Compare first token as command - Use atof()/atoi() for numeric values - Keep it robust: ignore lines that are too long, require exact commands Example commands: - TOFF <float>  (temp offset C) - HOFF <float>  (humidity offset %) - POFF <float>  (pressure offset hPa) - LOG <0|1> - UNIT <C|F> - STATUS (print current settings) Implementation outline: - global buffer char line[64]; - index pointer - in loop: while(Serial.available()) read char - if '\n' or '\r' and buffer not empty => processLine() - else append if space available When printing status, note it will mess with Serial Plotter. So if you’re using Plotter, you may want to temporarily switch to Serial Monitor for commands, or accept that status lines will disrupt the plot. Another trick: prefix status lines with a non-numeric character; Plotter may ignore them depending on version, but not guaranteed. If you’re okay with that, here’s a compact parser you can integrate: char cmdLine[64]; uint8_t cmdPos = 0; void pollSerialCommands() { while (Serial.available() > 0) { char c = Serial.read(); if (c == '\n' || c == '\r') { if (cmdPos > 0) { cmdLine[cmdPos] = '\0'; handleCommand(cmdLine); cmdPos = 0; } } else { if (cmdPos < sizeof(cmdLine) - 1) { cmdLine[cmdPos++] = c; } else { // overflow: reset line cmdPos = 0; } } } } void handleCommand(char *line) { // Tokenize char *cmd = strtok(line, " "); if (!cmd) return; if (strcmp(cmd, "TOFF") == 0) { char *arg = strtok(NULL, " "); if (arg) tempOffsetC = atof(arg); } else if (strcmp(cmd, "HOFF") == 0) { char *arg = strtok(NULL, " "); if (arg) humOffsetPct = atof(arg); } else if (strcmp(cmd, "POFF") == 0) { char *arg = strtok(NULL, " "); if (arg) pressOffsethPa = atof(arg); } else if (strcmp(cmd, "LOG") == 0) { char *arg = strtok(NULL, " "); if (arg) loggingEnabled = (atoi(arg) != 0); } else if (strcmp(cmd, "UNIT") == 0) { char *arg = strtok(NULL, " "); if (arg) { if (arg[0] == 'F' || arg[0] == 'f') useFahrenheit = true; if (arg[0] == 'C' || arg[0] == 'c') useFahrenheit = false; } } else if (strcmp(cmd, "STATUS") == 0) { Serial.print("# TOFF="); Serial.print(tempOffsetC, 3); Serial.print(" HOFF="); Serial.print(humOffsetPct, 3); Serial.print(" POFF="); Serial.print(pressOffsethPa, 3); Serial.print(" LOG="); Serial.print(loggingEnabled ? 1 : 0); Serial.print(" UNIT="); Serial.println(useFahrenheit ? "F" : "C"); } else { Serial.print("# Unknown cmd: "); Serial.println(cmd); } } Then call pollSerialCommands() near the top of loop(): pollSerialCommands(); If you want to keep Serial Plotter clean, you can: - Only print status lines when a command is received (as above) - Prefix with '#', so at least it’s clearly not data. If you later add EEPROM storage, you can persist offsets across resets.
Good point about EEPROM. Can we store offsets and settings so they persist? I don’t want to recalibrate every time.

Yes. The Uno has EEPROM you can write to. The key is: - Define a struct with your settings - Include a “magic number” and version so you know if EEPROM contains valid data - Use EEPROM.get() / EEPROM.put() (these handle writing only changed bytes in newer cores, but still minimize writes yourself) Example: #include <EEPROM.h> struct Settings { uint32_t magic; float tempOffsetC; float humOffsetPct; float pressOffsethPa; uint8_t useFahrenheit; uint8_t loggingEnabled; }; const uint32_t SETTINGS_MAGIC = 0xBEEF1234; Settings settings; void loadSettings() { EEPROM.get(0, settings); if (settings.magic != SETTINGS_MAGIC) { // defaults settings.magic = SETTINGS_MAGIC; settings.tempOffsetC = 0.0; settings.humOffsetPct = 0.0; settings.pressOffsethPa = 0.0; settings.useFahrenheit = 0; settings.loggingEnabled = 1; EEPROM.put(0, settings); } tempOffsetC = settings.tempOffsetC; humOffsetPct = settings.humOffsetPct; pressOffsethPa = settings.pressOffsethPa; useFahrenheit = (settings.useFahrenheit != 0); loggingEnabled = (settings.loggingEnabled != 0); } void saveSettings() { settings.magic = SETTINGS_MAGIC; settings.tempOffsetC = tempOffsetC; settings.humOffsetPct = humOffsetPct; settings.pressOffsethPa = pressOffsethPa; settings.useFahrenheit = useFahrenheit ? 1 : 0; settings.loggingEnabled = loggingEnabled ? 1 : 0; EEPROM.put(0, settings); } Then: - Call loadSettings() in setup after Serial begins. - In your handleCommand(), after changing a setting, call saveSettings(). - Also after button toggles (unit/log), call saveSettings() but maybe not on every press bounce. Since we already debounce and only toggle on press events, it’s fine. EEPROM endurance is typically ~100k writes per cell. If you save on every sample, that’s bad; but saving only on button press or command change is fine. If you want to be extra careful, you can add a “SAVE” command and only write when requested, but it’s usually okay to auto-save on change.
Understood. One more hardware question: I want to power it from a USB power bank. Any concerns with SD card writes and power stability?

Main concerns: 1) Sudden power loss during SD write If power drops while writing, the file system can corrupt, especially if the file is open. Your open-write-close approach reduces risk, but corruption is still possible. Mitigations: - Keep write operations short (you already do). - Consider writing to a new file per session (e.g., increment filename), so corruption affects only one file. - Add a capacitor on 5V rail to ride through tiny dips (e.g., 470 µF to 1000 µF electrolytic + 0.1 µF ceramic near SD module). This can help with SD current bursts. - Use a power bank that doesn’t shut off at low current draw. Some banks turn off if current is too low; adding an LCD/backlight usually prevents that, but a bare Uno + sensor might be borderline. 2) Current spikes SD cards can draw tens to 100+ mA momentarily. The Uno’s 3.3V pin is not great for powering SD directly; but your SD module is powered from 5V and has its own regulator, which is better. 3) Grounding and wiring Keep SD wiring short. Poor connections can look like “power issues.” 4) Brown-out / reset behavior If the Uno resets during a write, you might get partial lines. You can detect this later by checking CSV formatting. If you want, we can implement: - A session ID line at start - A periodic “flush marker” line - Or a simple “file sync” strategy But for a beginner build, your current approach is reasonable.
I like the session idea. Could we create a new file each boot, like LOG0001.CSV, LOG0002.CSV, etc.?

Yes. The standard SD library uses 8.3 filenames, so something like LOG0001.CSV fits. Strategy: - On boot, scan for the first unused filename. - Create it and write header. - Store the chosen filename in a global char array. Implementation: char filename[13]; // "LOG0001.CSV" + null void pickNewLogFilename() { for (int i = 0; i < 10000; i++) { sprintf(filename, "LOG%04d.CSV", i); if (!SD.exists(filename)) { return; } } // If all taken, fall back strcpy(filename, "LOG9999.CSV"); } Then in setup after SD.begin: pickNewLogFilename(); File f = SD.open(filename, FILE_WRITE); write header, close. And in logging: File f = SD.open(filename, FILE_WRITE); This avoids overwriting and isolates corruption to one session. Note: sprintf on AVR works, but float formatting in sprintf doesn’t by default; we’re only using it for integer filename, so it’s fine. Also, SD.exists() is somewhat slow, but scanning up to a few hundred files is okay. If you have thousands, boot time increases. A faster approach is storing the last used index in EEPROM, but scanning is simplest. If you want EEPROM approach: - Store nextLogIndex in EEPROM and increment each boot. - That’s one EEPROM write per boot, which is fine.
Let’s keep scanning for now. By the way, I’m curious: why did tying CS to VIN matter for I2C mode? I thought CS was only for SPI.

On combo I2C/SPI sensors, the “CS” (chip select) pin often doubles as an interface-mode selector: - If CS is HIGH at power-up (or always), the chip uses I2C. - If CS is LOW, it uses SPI. So even though “CS” is used in SPI transactions, its level also tells the chip which interface to listen on. If it’s floating, it can pick up noise and end up in SPI mode, meaning it won’t respond to I2C address probes—exactly what you saw. The BME280 specifically has a pin commonly called CSB. For I2C mode it should be pulled HIGH. Many breakouts include a pull-up resistor, but apparently yours either doesn’t, or the pull-up is weak and floating plus breadboard wiring made it unreliable. The SDO pin also has a dual role: - In SPI: it’s MISO data out. - In I2C: it sets the least significant bit of the address (0x76 vs 0x77). That’s why tying SDO to GND gave you 0x76.
That clears it up. Now I want to add a rain sensor later, maybe tipping bucket. That’s digital pulses. Any suggestion on how to integrate counting pulses while still doing the rest?

For a tipping-bucket rain gauge, you count switch closures (pulses). Two approaches: 1) Interrupt-based pulse counting (best for accuracy) - Use an external interrupt pin (Uno: D2 or D3). - Each pulse triggers an ISR that increments a counter. - In the main loop, periodically read and reset the counter to compute rainfall. Since you already use D2 and D3 for buttons, you’d need to move buttons to other pins (polling works fine on any digital pin), and reserve D2 or D3 for the rain gauge interrupt. 2) Polling with debounce (works if pulses are slow) - If your tipping bucket is slow (it usually is), you can poll it in loop with debounce and edge detection. - Interrupts are still cleaner. If you’re not ready for interrupts, you can still do polling reliably because a tipping bucket might tip at most a few times per second in very heavy rain. But you must ensure your loop doesn’t block (you’ve already removed delay) and SD writes don’t take too long. SD writes can occasionally take tens of milliseconds, which could cause missed pulses if you poll. So I’d recommend learning a simple interrupt for this one sensor. The ISR can be tiny: volatile unsigned long tipCount = 0; volatile unsigned long lastTipMicros = 0; void IRAM_ATTR onTip() { ... } // (IRAM_ATTR is ESP-specific, ignore on Uno) On Uno: void onTip() { unsigned long now = micros(); // debounce: ignore if within 5 ms if (now - lastTipMicros > 5000) { tipCount++; lastTipMicros = now; } } In setup: pinMode(RAIN_PIN, INPUT_PULLUP); attachInterrupt(digitalPinToInterrupt(RAIN_PIN), onTip, FALLING); Then in loop every sample: noInterrupts(); unsigned long tips = tipCount; tipCount = 0; interrupts(); Convert tips to rainfall: depends on gauge calibration (e.g., 0.2794 mm per tip, etc.) If you want to keep buttons on D2/D3, you can put rain gauge on a pin-change interrupt (more advanced) or swap: move buttons to D4/D5 and use D2 for rain.
I’ll probably move buttons. For wind speed, I’ve seen anemometers that output pulses too. Would the same interrupt approach work for both?

Yes. Wind speed sensors that use a reed switch typically output pulses proportional to rotation speed. You can count pulses per time interval. On Uno you have only two external interrupts (D2, D3). If you need both rain and wind with external interrupts, that fits perfectly: - D2: wind speed pulses - D3: rain tips pulses Buttons can move to other pins and be polled. You’d implement two ISRs, two counters. For wind direction, many vane sensors output an analog voltage through a resistor network, so you read it on an analog pin and map to directions. So a common weather station pin plan on Uno: - I2C: BME280 (+ LCD via level shifter later) - SPI: SD card - D2 interrupt: wind speed - D3 interrupt: rain - A0: wind direction analog - D4/D5: buttons - Possibly D6: status LED If you want to keep SD stable, avoid using D13 for anything else (it’s SPI clock and also LED). It’s okay but don’t attach heavy loads.
Speaking of the built-in LED on D13, will it interfere with SPI?

On the Uno, the onboard LED is connected to D13 through a resistor. D13 is also SCK for SPI. Normally it doesn’t prevent SPI from working, but: - It adds a small load/capacitance which can slightly degrade signal integrity at high speeds. - With typical SD modules and short wires, it’s usually fine. If you encounter SD reliability issues: - Keep wires short - Try a lower SPI speed (using SdFat library can help) - Ensure good power decoupling The D13 LED is rarely the main problem, but it can contribute in marginal setups.
I think this gives me a lot to work on. Before we wrap, can you explain briefly why SD modules sometimes fail to initialize? I see a lot of posts about it.

Common causes of SD init failures on Arduino Uno: 1) Wrong wiring (most common) - MOSI/MISO/SCK swapped - CS pin mismatch - Not sharing ground 2) Wrong voltage / level shifting - SD cards are 3.3V. If you use a bare SD socket without level shifting and feed 5V SPI, it can fail or be flaky. - Some “modules” have poor level shifting (resistor dividers) that can distort signals at higher speeds. 3) Power supply issues - SD cards draw current spikes; a weak 3.3V regulator or long wires can cause dips. - Powering SD from the Uno’s 3.3V pin is often unreliable. 4) Chip select not handled correctly - If multiple SPI devices share the bus, all non-selected devices must have their CS high, otherwise they may drive MISO and break communication. - On Uno, pin 10 must be set as OUTPUT to keep SPI in master mode (even if CS is on another pin). 5) Card formatting / filesystem - SD library expects FAT16/FAT32. exFAT cards (common on larger cards) won’t work with the basic SD library. - Some very large or very new cards can be problematic with older libraries. 6) Long wires / breadboards - SPI is fast; breadboards and long jumpers can cause ringing and signal integrity problems. If you hit issues, the troubleshooting order I’d use: - Confirm wiring with a known SD example (CardInfo) - Try a smaller, known-good card formatted FAT32 - Add decoupling capacitor near the module - Shorten wires - Consider SdFat library for better diagnostics
